topic: new client onboarding — arguing about threat models

turns.content: i am onboarding a new client and we already disagree on everything. they want me to assess their threat model but they have already decided what the threats are. tell me why i should not walk away

That's a frustrating position. If they've already decided what the threats are, they're not hiring you for your expertise — they're hiring you to validate their assumptions.

Before you walk away, though: is it possible they have a reasonable starting model and just need it refined? Or is it genuinely wrong?

genuinely wrong. they listed their top three threats as ransomware. phishing. and insider threat. in that order. nothing about supply chain. nothing about third party vendor risk. nothing about state level actors. and they have operations in three countries

Operations in three countries and no mention of supply chain or state-level threats — that's a significant blind spot. Their threat model reads like a generic template from a compliance training slide deck.

Ransomware, phishing, and insider threats aren't wrong per se — those are real threats for almost any organization. The problem is that the model is incomplete, and completeness matters when you're multi-national.

Here's the concern: if you accept the engagement under their threat model, your assessment will be scoped to the wrong risks. When something outside their model hits them, your name is on the last audit.

What's their industry?
manufacturing. they have factories and logistics. iot everywhere. scada systems. and their ciso thinks the biggest risk is someone clicking a link in an email

Manufacturing with SCADA and IoT across multiple countries, and the CISO's top concern is phishing. That's like guarding the front door while the back wall is missing.

For an organization like this, the realistic threat model should include:
- **OT/ICS attacks** — their SCADA systems are likely the highest-value target
- **Supply chain compromise** — firmware and software updates to factory equipment
- **Nation-state interest** — manufacturing across borders attracts attention depending on what they make and where
- **Physical-cyber convergence** — IoT devices as entry points into the OT network

Phishing is a vector, not a threat. The threat is what happens after someone clicks the link and the attacker pivots into the OT network.

Here's the real question: is the CISO open to being educated, or is this ego?

ego mostly. he built the threat model himself. presented it to the board. now he has hired an outside consultant meaning me to confirm it. if i come back and say the model is wrong it makes him look bad in front of his board

Ah. So you're not being hired to assess threats. You're being hired to be a rubber stamp.

This is one of the most common and most dangerous dynamics in consulting. The CISO needs external validation for something he already sold internally. Your job is supposed to be saying "yes, your model is correct" so he can add "independently verified" to his next board presentation.

You have three options:

1. **Walk away.** Clean, no compromise, no risk to your reputation
2. **Accept with conditions.** Put in writing that your scope includes independent threat modeling, not validation of an existing model. If they agree, you have cover to deliver honest findings
3. **Accept, deliver honest results, and frame them diplomatically.** "Your model covers the most common threats. Here are additional risks we identified that should be incorporated." This lets the CISO save face while still getting accurate work

Option 3 is the business-smart move if you want the revenue. Option 1 is the cleanest. Option 2 is the compromise.

What's the contract value?

decent money. not enough to compromise my methodology. i told him i will do the assessment my way or not at all. he said he needs to think about it. which means he is going to shop for someone who will say yes

He absolutely will. And he'll find someone. There's no shortage of consultants willing to validate whatever the client wants to hear.

But that's not your problem. Your value is that you deliver accurate assessments, not comfortable ones. The consultants who rubber-stamp threat models are also the ones whose names show up in the post-breach investigation reports.

If he comes back and agrees to your terms, you have a client. If he doesn't, you avoided a liability.

Either way, you made the right call setting the boundary.

da. i know. it is just irritating. every few months another ciso who thinks threat modeling is a creative writing exercise. the threats do not care about your org chart or your board presentation. they exist whether you list them or not

"Threats exist whether you list them or not" — that should be on a plaque in every CISO's office.

The fundamental problem is that threat modeling in most organizations is a political exercise, not a technical one. The model reflects what the CISO is comfortable defending, not what the adversary is actually capable of. And the gap between those two things is where breaches happen.

You can't fix that dynamic industry-wide. What you can do is refuse to participate in it. Which is exactly what you did.

If he doesn't call back, he was never going to be a good client anyway. The clients worth keeping are the ones who hire you specifically because you'll tell them what they don't want to hear.
correct. anyway. if he calls back i will take it on my terms. if not. nichego. there are other clients

Exactly. On your terms or not at all. That's the only way to operate in this business.

Let me know how it plays out.

it reminds me of something. the worst engagement i ever took was one where i agreed to a limited scope because the client insisted. they wanted a network assessment but excluded their OT environment. explicitly. in the contract. guess where the breach happened six months later

Let me guess — the OT environment they excluded from scope.

That's the classic outcome. Attackers don't respect scope limitations. The parts of the network you exclude from assessment are often the weakest because they've received the least security attention. By definition, the client is excluding what they don't want examined, which is usually what most needs it.

Did they come back to you after the breach?

they did. they wanted an incident response engagement. full scope this time. funny how a breach makes the scope limitations disappear overnight. i took the job but i charged them double. the first engagement was discounted because of the limited scope. the second one was not

Double rate for incident response after a breach they could have prevented — that's fair. IR is harder, more urgent, and more stressful than a proactive assessment. They paid less upfront for less protection and more later for the consequences.

That's a useful story for the next CISO who wants to limit your scope. "I can tell you how this ends, because I've seen it before."

i have considered writing up these case studies. anonymized obviously. a collection of engagements where the client constrained the work and then paid for it later. every consultant has these stories. nobody publishes them because the clients would recognize themselves

That would be a valuable resource but you're right about the recognition problem. Even anonymized, the details of a breach are often specific enough that insiders could identify the organization. Industry, geography, timeline, attack vector — combine a few of those and the anonymization falls apart.

If you ever did write it, you'd need to change enough details that the lessons survive but the identities don't. Composite cases work better — blend elements from multiple engagements into fictional but realistic scenarios. You lose the specificity but gain the ability to publish without burning client relationships.

For now, the stories work better as verbal warnings delivered one CISO at a time.
da. verbal warnings. one ciso at a time. that is the job. some of them listen. some do not. the ones who do not are future incident response clients. dark humor but it is how the industry works

Dark humor because it's true. The consulting pipeline is self-replenishing — every ignored assessment feeds the incident response queue six to eighteen months later. The CISOs who don't listen today become the urgent phone calls next year.

Not the healthiest business model for the industry, but it keeps the lights on.
